back_about_text: Look all around — colours are everywhere! From the red pepper to the white goat, Dami finds every colour in the Nigerian compound.
Big things and small things are all around us. Can you find them? A big pot and a small cup, a big goat and a small snail — big and small are everywhere.
Naza is at the market with Mama. She sees a mat, a pot, and a yam — and she sees Mama too.
Ada has a red bag. What is inside? A book, a fan, a yam — the red bag is very full!
Emeka can run! He runs on the road, to the tree, and to the gate. Emeka can run and run.
Ada finds a goat in the yard — but the goat will not stay still! A funny story about a very busy goat.
Mama has a big pot on the fire. Naza can see it. The pot is hot! A quiet story about Mama's cooking fire.
What do you see? A yam, a mat, a pot, a drum — look around and you will find so many things to see.
The rain is coming! Run, run, run! Everything is getting wet — but in Nigeria, rain on the farm is always good.
The goat is up. The hen is up. The cat is up. And I am up too! A morning greeting for every creature in the yard.

book_title: One, Two, Three at the Market
back_about_text: One big yam, two red peppers, ten things at the market! A counting journey through a busy Nigerian market.
In, on, under, behind, in front, up, down — where is it? A playful look at position words using everyday Nigerian objects.
Kemi has her bag and she is ready for school. She runs down the road, finds her friend Ada, and walks through the school gate for the very first time.
Emeka says he can run. Ada says she can run too! They race across the yard to find out who is faster — and end up laughing together under the tree.
Mama's fan is gone — and Kemi knows where it is. A warm story about a girl, a fan, and a mama who is not angry.
Amina's kite is flying high, then it gets stuck in the tree. Can Emeka reach it? A story about a kite, a tree, and a good friend.
The rain comes fast and the mat gets wet! Mama and Naza run out to save it — and end up laughing at how wet they all are.
Boom, boom, boom! The drum is singing and everyone is dancing. A lively poem about the sound of the talking drum.
The goat, the hen, the cat — they all live in the yard. A cheerful poem about the animals a Nigerian child knows best.
We can run! We can jump! We can sing, drum, read, and write! A chant about everything children can do when they believe in themselves.
Ada goes to the yard and the red hen is gone. She searches everywhere — until a clucking sound leads her to a wonderful surprise.
eb_comprehension_skill: Cause and Effect / Reality vs. Fantasy

back_about_text: Bolu wants a big talking drum, just like Papa's. Papa says he must earn it. A story about a boy who works hard and gets what he deserves.
eb_comprehension_skill: Cause and Effect / Reality vs. Fantasy

back_about_text: Amina walks the long river road to visit her grandma in the dry season. She sees a lizard, a hawk, and a shea tree — and then, at last, Grandma by the riverbank.
eb_comprehension_skill: Cause and Effect / Reality vs. Fantasy

back_about_text: Naza goes with Papa to the farm at the start of the rainy season. When the rain comes hard, they shelter together and Papa explains why rain is good.
eb_comprehension_skill: Cause and Effect / Reality vs. Fantasy

back_about_text: Bisi is the bell monitor — but one morning the bell is gone! She must think carefully before the children waiting in the yard lose their patience.
eb_comprehension_skill: Cause and Effect / Reality vs. Fantasy

book_title: Why the Tortoise Has a Cracked Shell
back_about_text: Long ago, Tortoise had a smooth, bright shell. But his greed at a sky feast cost him everything. A classic West African tale about pride and what happens when you take too much.
eb_comprehension_skill: Cause and Effect / Reality vs. Fantasy

back_about_text: Where does water come from? From wells, rivers, taps, and rain. A clear look at how Nigerian families find and carry water through the dry and rainy seasons.
eb_comprehension_skill: Cause and Effect / Reality vs. Fantasy

back_about_text: From yam and cassava in the south to millet and sorghum in the north, Nigeria's farmers grow food for everyone. A look at what grows across this large and varied land.
eb_comprehension_skill: Cause and Effect / Reality vs. Fantasy

back_about_text: Dust in the air, dry grass, and cold mornings — the harmattan has come. A seasonal poem about Nigeria's famous dry wind, and the rains that always follow.
eb_comprehension_skill: Cause and Effect / Reality vs. Fantasy

back_about_text: Come to market! The road is full, the stalls are bright, and everything you need is here. A lively poem about the sights, sounds, and smells of market day.
eb_comprehension_skill: Cause and Effect / Reality vs. Fantasy

back_about_text: It is the day of the school play and Emeka cannot remember his words. His friend Ada gives him one piece of advice — and it changes everything.
Bolu's drum is missing. He asks Naza. He asks Amina. Amina smiles and says, come with me. What they find under the mango tree is very unexpected.
It is harvest day and Amina wakes before sunrise to go to the farm for the very first time. She works hard all morning and learns something important about where food comes from.
Naza leads Emeka and Ada to a place she has always known — a river just beyond the estate. A story about friendship, a kingfisher, and the things that are always there if you look.
Ada and Stella watch a large brown crab by the river. Stella knows its name, its food, and its importance. That evening, Ada draws it in her book.

book_title: How Anansi Got All the Stories
back_about_text: All the stories in the world belong to Nyame the Sky God — until a small spider decides to challenge him. The classic Anansi story, retold for young Nigerian readers.
Jollof rice is loved across Nigeria. Follow each step — from blending the tomatoes to lifting the lid — and learn how to make one of Nigeria's greatest dishes.
The keke napep is one of Nigeria's busiest vehicles. Find out where it came from, how it works, and why keke drivers know their neighbourhoods better than anyone.
The smell of rain on dry ground. Cold ogi in the morning. The mango tree at sunset. A personal poem about all the things a Nigerian child loves most.
Every word is a step. Every book is a road. A closing poem that traces the Tafiya journey from Tashi to Tafiya — and sends every reader forward.
